meta.notes: Same Saylor 'General Chemistry I MCQ' QuizOver PDF (CC BY-NC-ND 3.0) as cpbs-genchem-05. The PDF withholds the answer key (links to QuizOver.com), so all items are answerProvenance=verified-from-source-text with answers derived from established VSEPR/bonding rules, valence-electron counts, and gas laws. Choices reproduced verbatim in printed order, labeled A-D. Selected to add molecular-geometry/shape coverage (linear, trigonal bipyramidal, bent, etc.) and electronegativity trends not present in the GOB-derived files; deliberately avoided duplicating earlier bonding items. LICENSE NOTE: open but NC + ND; confirm rights before any commercial shipping.

questions.stem: A sample of a certain gas has a volume of 222 mL at 695 mm Hg and 0 °C. What would be the volume of this same sample of gas if it were measured at 333 mm Hg and 0 °C?
questions.explanation: Temperature is constant, so by Boyle's law V2 = V1 × P1/P2 = 222 mL × (695 mm Hg / 333 mm Hg) = 463 mL. Lowering the pressure increases the volume.
questions.sourceName: QuizOver.com / The Saylor Foundation — General Chemistry I MCQ (Smithback & Lerebours), Unit 05: States of Matter
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-ND 3.0 (The Saylor Foundation; NonCommercial, NoDerivatives)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim. The source PDF withholds the answer key; answer independently verified by calculation.

questions.stem: For a given sample of gas molecules, the average kinetic energy depends only on the value of the ________________.
questions.explanation: The average translational kinetic energy of gas molecules is directly proportional to the absolute temperature (KE_avg = 3/2 kT). It does not depend on pressure, volume, or the number of moles.
questions.sourceName: QuizOver.com / The Saylor Foundation — General Chemistry I MCQ (Smithback & Lerebours), Unit 05: States of Matter
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-ND 3.0 (The Saylor Foundation; NonCommercial, NoDerivatives)

questions.stem: The phase change from solid to gas is called ______________.
questions.explanation: Sublimation is the direct conversion of a solid to a gas without passing through the liquid phase (e.g., dry ice). Boiling and evaporation are liquid-to-gas transitions.
questions.sourceName: QuizOver.com / The Saylor Foundation — General Chemistry I MCQ (Smithback & Lerebours), Unit 05: States of Matter
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-ND 3.0 (The Saylor Foundation; NonCommercial, NoDerivatives)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim. The source PDF withholds the answer key; answer independently verified (definitional).

questions.stem: According to Valence Shell Electron Pair Repulsion Theory (VSEPR), what number of bonded atoms is normally expected to produce a tetrahedral geometry?
questions.explanation: A central atom with four bonding electron domains and no lone pairs adopts a tetrahedral geometry, with ideal bond angles of 109.5°.
questions.sourceName: QuizOver.com / The Saylor Foundation — General Chemistry I MCQ (Smithback & Lerebours), Unit 03: Bonding
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-ND 3.0 (The Saylor Foundation; NonCommercial, NoDerivatives)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim. The source PDF withholds the answer key; answer independently verified.

questions.stem: Based on the Valence Shell Electron Pair Repulsion Theory (VSEPR), what is the molecular geometry of BeCl2?
questions.explanation: Beryllium in BeCl2 has only two bonding domains and no lone pairs, so the molecule is linear (Cl-Be-Cl angle = 180°).
questions.sourceName: QuizOver.com / The Saylor Foundation — General Chemistry I MCQ (Smithback & Lerebours), Unit 03: Bonding
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-ND 3.0 (The Saylor Foundation; NonCommercial, NoDerivatives)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim. The source PDF withholds the answer key; answer independently verified.

questions.stem: Based on the Valence Shell Electron Pair Repulsion Theory (VSEPR), what is the molecular geometry of PI5?
questions.explanation: Phosphorus in PI5 has five bonding domains and no lone pairs, giving a trigonal bipyramidal geometry.
questions.sourceName: QuizOver.com / The Saylor Foundation — General Chemistry I MCQ (Smithback & Lerebours), Unit 03: Bonding
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-ND 3.0 (The Saylor Foundation; NonCommercial, NoDerivatives)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim. The source PDF withholds the answer key; answer independently verified.

questions.stem: Based on the Valence Shell Electron Pair Repulsion Theory (VSEPR), what is the molecular geometry of CF4?
questions.explanation: Carbon in CF4 has four bonding domains and no lone pairs, giving a tetrahedral geometry (bond angles 109.5°).
questions.sourceName: QuizOver.com / The Saylor Foundation — General Chemistry I MCQ (Smithback & Lerebours), Unit 03: Bonding
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-ND 3.0 (The Saylor Foundation; NonCommercial, NoDerivatives)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim. The source PDF withholds the answer key; answer independently verified.

questions.stem: Based on the Valence Shell Electron Pair Repulsion Theory (VSEPR), which of the following corresponds most closely to the geometry of the IBr2- ion?
questions.explanation: In IBr2- the central iodine has 2 bonding pairs and 3 lone pairs (5 electron domains). The three lone pairs occupy the equatorial positions of a trigonal bipyramid, leaving the two I-Br bonds axial, so the ion is linear.
questions.sourceName: QuizOver.com / The Saylor Foundation — General Chemistry I MCQ (Smithback & Lerebours), Unit 03: Bonding
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-ND 3.0 (The Saylor Foundation; NonCommercial, NoDerivatives)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim (subscript/charge formatting normalized). The source PDF withholds the answer key; answer independently verified.

questions.stem: Based on the Valence Shell Electron Pair Repulsion Theory (VSEPR), which of the following corresponds most closely to the molecular geometry of SF2?
questions.explanation: Sulfur in SF2 has 2 bonding pairs and 2 lone pairs (4 electron domains, tetrahedral electron geometry), giving a bent molecular shape with a bond angle near 109.5°.
questions.sourceName: QuizOver.com / The Saylor Foundation — General Chemistry I MCQ (Smithback & Lerebours), Unit 03: Bonding
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-ND 3.0 (The Saylor Foundation; NonCommercial, NoDerivatives)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim. The source PDF withholds the answer key; answer independently verified.

questions.stem: Eighteen electrons are present in the Lewis structure of which of the following molecules?
questions.explanation: Total valence electrons: SO2 = 6 + 2(6) = 18. By comparison, N2O = 2(5) + 6 = 16, CO2 = 4 + 2(6) = 16, and HCN = 1 + 4 + 5 = 10.
questions.sourceName: QuizOver.com / The Saylor Foundation — General Chemistry I MCQ (Smithback & Lerebours), Unit 03: Bonding
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-ND 3.0 (The Saylor Foundation; NonCommercial, NoDerivatives)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim. The source PDF withholds the answer key; answer independently verified by valence-electron count.

questions.stem: How many p orbitals are there (in a given p subshell)?
questions.explanation: A p subshell contains three orbitals (px, py, pz), oriented along the three perpendicular axes; together they hold up to six electrons.
questions.sourceName: QuizOver.com / The Saylor Foundation — General Chemistry I MCQ (Smithback & Lerebours), Unit 03: Bonding
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-ND 3.0 (The Saylor Foundation; NonCommercial, NoDerivatives)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim (parenthetical clarifier added; original stem 'How many p orbitals are there?'). The source PDF withholds the answer key; answer independently verified.

questions.stem: In which of the following pairs of molecules do both members of the pair have the same molecular geometry?
questions.explanation: H2O and OF2 each have a central atom with two bonding pairs and two lone pairs, so both are bent. In the other pairs the members differ (SO2 bent vs CO2 linear; H2S bent vs HCN linear; NH3 trigonal pyramidal vs SO3 trigonal planar).
questions.sourceName: QuizOver.com / The Saylor Foundation — General Chemistry I MCQ (Smithback & Lerebours), Unit 03: Bonding
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-ND 3.0 (The Saylor Foundation; NonCommercial, NoDerivatives)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim. The source PDF withholds the answer key; answer independently verified.

questions.stem: What is the approximate H-B-H angle in BH3?
questions.explanation: BH3 is trigonal planar (three bonding domains, no lone pairs on boron), so the H-B-H bond angles are about 120°.
questions.sourceName: QuizOver.com / The Saylor Foundation — General Chemistry I MCQ (Smithback & Lerebours), Unit 03: Bonding
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-ND 3.0 (The Saylor Foundation; NonCommercial, NoDerivatives)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim (original option 3 printed '120'; normalized to 120°). The source PDF withholds the answer key; answer independently verified.

questions.stem: What is the hybridization of the oxygen atom in water?

choices.label: D
choices.text: The oxygen atom does not hybridize in water.

questions.explanation: Oxygen in water has two bonding pairs and two lone pairs (four electron domains), which corresponds to sp3 hybridization and a bent molecular shape.
questions.sourceName: QuizOver.com / The Saylor Foundation — General Chemistry I MCQ (Smithback & Lerebours), Unit 03: Bonding
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-ND 3.0 (The Saylor Foundation; NonCommercial, NoDerivatives)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim. The source PDF withholds the answer key; answer independently verified.

questions.stem: Which molecule contains a double bond?
questions.explanation: Phosgene, COCl2, has a carbon-oxygen double bond (C=O) plus two C-Cl single bonds. SF2 and CF4 contain only single bonds.
questions.sourceName: QuizOver.com / The Saylor Foundation — General Chemistry I MCQ (Smithback & Lerebours), Unit 03: Bonding
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-ND 3.0 (The Saylor Foundation; NonCommercial, NoDerivatives)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim. The source PDF withholds the answer key; answer independently verified.

questions.stem: Which of the following best describes the variation of the electronegativity of the elements with respect to their position on the periodic table?

choices.label: A
choices.text: increases across a period; increases down a group

choices.label: B
choices.text: increases across a period; decreases down a group

choices.label: C
choices.text: decreases across a period; increases down a group

choices.label: D
choices.text: decreases across a period; decreases down a group

questions.explanation: Electronegativity increases from left to right across a period (increasing nuclear charge, smaller atoms) and decreases down a group (valence electrons farther from the nucleus, more shielding).
questions.sourceName: QuizOver.com / The Saylor Foundation — General Chemistry I MCQ (Smithback & Lerebours), Unit 03: Bonding
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-ND 3.0 (The Saylor Foundation; NonCommercial, NoDerivatives)

questions.stem: Which of the following combinations of two elements is most likely to produce highly ionic bonds?
questions.explanation: Ionic character increases with the electronegativity difference between the elements. Lithium (a low-electronegativity metal) combined with fluorine (the most electronegative element) gives the most ionic bond. The other pairs are nonmetal-nonmetal (covalent).
questions.sourceName: QuizOver.com / The Saylor Foundation — General Chemistry I MCQ (Smithback & Lerebours), Unit 03: Bonding
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-ND 3.0 (The Saylor Foundation; NonCommercial, NoDerivatives)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim. The source PDF withholds the answer key; answer independently verified.

questions.stem: Which of the following combinations of two elements is most likely to produce covalent bonds?
questions.explanation: Covalent bonds form between two nonmetals of similar electronegativity. Nitrogen and oxygen are both nonmetals, so they bond covalently. The other pairs each contain a reactive metal (Na or Li) with a nonmetal, favoring ionic bonding.
questions.sourceName: QuizOver.com / The Saylor Foundation — General Chemistry I MCQ (Smithback & Lerebours), Unit 03: Bonding
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-ND 3.0 (The Saylor Foundation; NonCommercial, NoDerivatives)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim. The source PDF withholds the answer key; answer independently verified.

questions.stem: Which of the following elements is most likely to participate in the formation of multiple bonds?
questions.explanation: Among these, sulfur most readily forms multiple (double) bonds, as in SO2 and SO3. Hydrogen forms only a single bond, sodium bonds ionically as a metal, and chlorine typically forms single bonds.
questions.sourceName: QuizOver.com / The Saylor Foundation — General Chemistry I MCQ (Smithback & Lerebours), Unit 03: Bonding
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-ND 3.0 (The Saylor Foundation; NonCommercial, NoDerivatives)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim. The source PDF withholds the answer key; answer independently verified.

questions.stem: Which of the following elements is most likely to form compounds involving an expanded valence shell of electrons?
questions.explanation: Phosphorus is a period-3 element with accessible d orbitals, so it can exceed an octet (e.g., PCl5, PF6-). Period-2 atoms O and N cannot expand their valence shell, and Na is a metal that loses electrons.
